questions.text: I sense what someone needs to hear in a given moment based on their nonverbal cues.
I am attracted to opportunities where I can blend into a well-functioning team.
I find that my emotional reactions are louder and more persistent than I'd choose if I could choose.
I notice that strong emotions make me hyper-aware of my heartbeat, as if my body is broadcasting its state.
I hold the view that I deserve to enjoy the lifestyle my work has earned.
I often cancel my own plans to help a friend, colleague, or family member.
I notice that I can work through emotional challenges without losing productivity.
I tend to let others choose first—the restaurant, the movie, the vacation destination.
I tend to focus on what was decided in a meeting rather than on the interpersonal dynamics that influenced the decision.
I believe that objectivity about suffering is what makes it possible to help many people rather than just the ones whose pain is most visible.
I am the person who turns a vague idea into a concrete plan of action.
I feel that the weight I assign to others' suffering in my decisions is the most honest indicator of what I value.
I value predictability in my daily routine because it lets me focus on what matters.
I maintain a structured approach to professional development with clear learning objectives.
I tend to be surprised when someone tells me about interpersonal tension I had not noticed.
I think that objective analysis of suffering, free from emotional overlay, is what allows for truly impartial and just responses.
I tend to direct the flow of conversation toward outcomes I envision.
I make each recurring decision fresh because circumstances always differ.
I hold the view that composure in the face of others' pain is a practical virtue that creates the conditions under which effective help becomes possible.
I am the type who helps the group discover its own rules of engagement.
I buy supplies when I run out rather than stockpiling in advance.
I hold the view that most communication happens in what is left unsaid.
I often help the group sit with ambiguity until clarity emerges together.
I notice that I don't get swept up in the emotional energy of crowds or groups.
I routinely check in on neighbors, acquaintances, and distant friends to make sure they are okay.
I feel that the willingness to sacrifice personal comfort in response to another's pain is a defining feature of a morally serious person.
I often do not notice when a compliment carries a double meaning.
I notice the way a person's face subtly changes when a sensitive subject comes up.
I tend to see helping others as secondary to achieving my own objectives.
I notice that my emotional intensity ebbs and flows like weather systems rather than following a predictable pattern.
I routinely evaluate friendships by whether they contribute to my personal development.
I confirm appointments the day before to avoid miscommunications.
I believe that every instance of suffering places a moral demand on those who are aware of it, including me.
I find that I can receive life-changing news and still feel grounded in my body.
I feel that if I don't take the reins, things won't move forward.
I hold my commitments loosely, knowing that priorities shift and flexibility matters.
